Post-Construction Window Cleaning in the Shuswap: What New Build Windows Actually Need

Published 2026-05-29 · Wagon Windows

Moving into a new home in Salmon Arm or Armstrong is exciting — but the windows that look fine from a distance often tell a different story up close. Construction dust, paint overspray, sticker adhesive, and silicone residue are nearly universal on new build windows, and they require a very different approach than routine cleaning. Getting this first clean right sets the baseline for every clean that follows.

Need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Get an Instant Estimate (403) 837-5092

What's Actually on New Build Windows (It's Not Just Dust)

Post-construction contamination on windows is a mix of several distinct substances, each with different chemistry and removal requirements. Understanding what you're dealing with explains why a standard window cleaning isn't the right tool for the job. Construction dust is the most visible issue, but it's also the most dangerous for glass. Drywall dust, concrete dust, and silica from cutting operations are all abrasive. The key risk isn't the dust itself — it's what happens when abrasive dust gets trapped under a squeegee or scrubber and is dragged across the glass surface. This is how new windows get scratched during improper post-construction cleaning. The pre-rinse step that professional cleaners use isn't ceremonial — it's the most important scratch-prevention measure on a new build job. Paint overspray is the second major contaminant. Trade painters rarely mask every inch of glass, and spray drift reaches surprisingly far. On most new builds, there will be fine paint dots, splatter patterns, or edge runs on at least some windows. Water-based latex paint overspray, if caught fresh, rinses off. Older paint or oil-based products need controlled scraping with a fresh razor blade held at the correct angle. Done wrong, this scratches glass. Done right, it removes paint cleanly without a mark. Label adhesive from manufacturer stickers is nearly universal on new windows. Some builders leave these on for protection during construction; others don't bother removing them before handover. The adhesive under labels is designed for permanence, and soap and water don't touch it. Proper adhesive removal requires a solvent step — most professionals use an orange-oil based product — before the standard clean.

  • Construction dust: abrasive, must be pre-rinsed before any scrubbing to prevent scratches
  • Paint overspray: requires controlled razor blade removal with proper technique
  • Label adhesive: solvent-based removal required before water-based cleaning
  • Silicone caulk residue: leftover from sealing frames, requires specific solvents
  • Grout haze: present on windows near tile work, needs acid-based or mechanical removal

Why New Build Windows Are Scratch-Risk Situations

New build windows occupy a risk category that experienced window cleaners treat differently from standard residential maintenance cleans. The scratch risk is genuinely elevated, for reasons that aren't always obvious. Tempered glass — standard on most patio doors, side lites, and lower windows in modern construction — has a phenomenon called 'fabricating debris' or 'glass scratching syndrome.' During the manufacturing process, tiny glass particles can become embedded in the surface of tempered glass. These particles aren't visible to the naked eye, but when a scrubber or squeegee passes over them, they act like embedded sandpaper and create fine scratches. This is not a cleaning error — it's a manufacturing quality issue — but the scratches show up during the first clean, and untangling responsibility requires understanding this phenomenon. The practical implication: professional post-construction cleaners should inspect tempered glass carefully, use soft scrubbers rather than steel wool or abrasive pads, and document the condition of each pane before starting. If fabricating debris is present, mechanical scrubbing on that glass creates scratches that aren't the cleaner's fault but are the cleaner's problem to manage. Beyond fabricating debris, the combination of construction grit and new glass simply demands caution. A window that's been through a building site has been exposed to conditions that standard window cleaning technique doesn't account for. Pre-rinsing with low pressure to float off loose abrasive, then applying solution, then cleaning — rather than the standard scrub-immediately approach — is the right sequence for new builds.

Ask any professional you hire whether they've done post-construction cleans before and what their scratch-prevention protocol is. A vague answer is a red flag.

Salmon Arm and Armstrong New Build Context

The Shuswap region — particularly Salmon Arm and Armstrong — has seen sustained residential construction growth over the past several years. Housing demand from people relocating from the Lower Mainland and Alberta has driven new development in areas like Grandview Bench, North Broadview, and expanding subdivisions on the hillside above downtown Salmon Arm. Armstrong's agricultural land base and lower lot costs have made it a target for new residential projects as well. This construction activity means post-construction window cleaning is not a rare specialty job in this region — it's a regular part of the seasonal workload. New build homeowners in the Shuswap face a few factors that make their situation specific: Wildfire smoke season (typically July through September) can deposit a fine particulate film on windows during construction if the build spans a summer. This adds an additional layer to the post-construction contamination that isn't always present in other regions. Hard water from municipal systems (and especially from well water on rural properties near Salmon Arm) means that any residual moisture on new windows during construction can leave mineral deposits on glass that's already contaminated with construction dust. These deposits mineralize quickly and require acid-based treatment to remove. New builds that back onto agricultural land — common in the Armstrong area — face seasonal dust exposure from field operations: tilling, harvesting, and irrigation system spray all deposit material on windows during construction.

  • Grandview Bench and North Broadview among active residential development areas in Salmon Arm
  • Armstrong: growing new residential activity driven by land availability and lower lot costs
  • Wildfire smoke particulate is a regional factor for builds completed through summer
  • Hard water and agricultural dust compound post-construction contamination in this area

What to Ask Your Builder About Window Cleaning Responsibility

One of the most common sources of confusion for new build homeowners is who pays for the post-construction window clean. The answer depends entirely on what's in your purchase contract or builder's warranty, and it's worth clarifying before you close. Many production builders include a 'construction clean' in their delivery package, but this is typically performed by a general cleaning crew, not window specialists. A construction clean usually means vacuuming, wiping surfaces, and mopping — not the specialist post-construction window cleaning we're describing here. If you ask your builder what their window cleaning process is and the answer is 'our cleaning crew handles it,' it's worth asking specifically whether they remove paint overspray, adhesive residue, and construction dust from glass with appropriate specialty products. Some premium builders include a proper specialist post-construction window clean in the delivery package. If yours does, confirm that the company they use has post-construction experience — not just standard residential experience. If the builder's clean doesn't include specialist window care, budget for it separately. On a typical Shuswap new build with 20-35 windows, a proper post-construction clean typically runs more than a standard residential maintenance clean, reflecting the additional time, specialized products, and higher skill level required. It's a one-time cost that's worth paying correctly rather than cutting corners on. Get clarity on this in writing before the handover walkthrough. Walking through a new home and noting window condition in the deficiency list — if the builder's clean hasn't addressed the glass adequately — is your documentation for getting it resolved under warranty.

  • Ask specifically whether the builder's clean includes paint overspray and adhesive removal
  • General construction cleaning ≠ specialist post-construction window cleaning
  • Document window condition in the handover deficiency list if standard of clean is inadequate
  • Budget post-construction specialist cleaning as a separate line if builder doesn't include it

The First Clean Sets the Baseline for Everything After

Here's why the post-construction clean deserves more attention and budget than any subsequent routine clean: it establishes the starting point for the entire life of those windows. If paint overspray isn't fully removed and gets left to harden, it becomes progressively harder to clean over time. The same is true for silicone residue and adhesive. These substances bond more strongly to glass with every freeze-thaw cycle and season of UV exposure. A post-construction clean done six months after move-in is significantly harder than one done at handover. More importantly, any contamination that remains on glass after the first clean gets 'overcoated' by subsequent cleaning. Regular window cleaning — standard squeegee and solution work — doesn't penetrate bonded paint or silicone residue. Each subsequent clean makes the glass look cleaner in the moment, but the underlying contamination is still there, and eventually it becomes visible as a persistent haze or patch that won't clean out. For homeowners investing in a new Shuswap build, the post-construction clean is the one appointment that protects the quality of the glass for the life of the home. Do it right, do it soon after handover, and do it with someone who knows the difference between a post-construction clean and a standard residential visit. Wagon Windows handles post-construction window cleaning in Salmon Arm, Armstrong, and throughout the Shuswap. We assess each job before starting, document pane condition, and use the right product sequence for each contaminant type. Call David at (403) 837-5092 to discuss your new build.

  • Bonded paint and silicone harden over time and become progressively harder to remove
  • Standard routine cleaning does not penetrate bonded post-construction residue
  • Best timing: as soon as possible after builder handover, before first winter
  • A proper first clean protects glass quality for the life of the home

Frequently Asked Questions

How is post-construction window cleaning different from regular window cleaning?

Post-construction cleaning addresses contaminants that standard window cleaning doesn't touch: paint overspray, label adhesive, silicone caulk residue, and abrasive construction dust. It requires solvent pre-treatment for adhesives, controlled razor blade work for paint, and a careful pre-rinse protocol to avoid dragging abrasive grit across glass surfaces. It typically takes longer and costs more than a routine maintenance clean.

Can I clean new build windows myself to save money?

For basic dust removal on easily accessible windows, yes. But paint overspray and adhesive removal involve chemistry and technique that's easy to get wrong. Improper razor blade use on tempered glass and the wrong solvent on certain window films or coatings can cause permanent damage. For anything beyond light dust, a specialist is worth the cost — especially given the investment in a new home.

My builder says the windows were cleaned. Why do they still look dirty?

Builder delivery cleans are usually general construction cleans, not specialist window cleaning. The crew wipes down surfaces but typically doesn't remove paint overspray, adhesive residue, or do the specialist pre-rinse work required for construction-contaminated glass. This is common across the industry. Request a specialist post-construction clean as a deficiency item, or budget for it independently.

How soon after moving in should I get a post-construction window clean?

As soon as possible after handover — ideally within the first month. Paint overspray and adhesive residue are easier to remove fresh. Waiting through a winter allows freeze-thaw cycles to bond contaminants more firmly to the glass. If your build was completed in fall, aim to get the clean done before the first hard freeze.
